candidate.foreshadow_text:
Sue looked solicitously out the window. What was there to count? There was only a bare, dreary yard to be seen, and the blank side of the brick house twenty feet away. An old, old ivy vine, gnarled and decayed at the roots, climbed half way up the brick wall. The cold breath of autumn had stricken its leaves from the vine until its skeleton branches clung, almost bare, to the crumbling bricks.

“What is it, dear?” asked Sue.

“Six,” said Johnsy, in almost a whisper. “They’re falling faster now. Three days ago there were almost a hundred. It made my head ache to count them. But now it’s easy. There goes another one. There are only five left now.”

“Five what, dear. Tell your Sudie.”

“Leaves. On the ivy vine. When the last one falls I must go, too. I’ve known that for three days. Didn’t the doctor tell you?”

“Oh, I never heard of such nonsense,” complained Sue, with magnificent scorn. “What have old ivy leaves to do with your getting well? And you used to love that vine so, you naughty girl. Don’t be a goosey. Why, the doctor told me this morning that your chances for getting well real soon were—let’s see exactly what he said—he said the chances were ten to one! Why, that’s almost as good a chance as we have in New York when we ride on the street cars or walk past a new building. Try to take some broth now, and let Sudie go back to her drawing, so she can sell the editor man with it, and buy port wine for her sick child, and pork chops for her greedy self.”

“You needn’t get any more wine,” said Johnsy, keeping her eyes fixed out the window. “There goes another. No, I don’t want any broth. That leaves just four. I want to see the last one fall before it gets dark. Then I’ll go, too.”
candidate.payoff_text: “I have something to tell you, white mouse,” she said. “Mr. Behrman died of pneumonia to-day in the hospital. He was ill only two days. The janitor found him on the morning of the first day in his room downstairs helpless with pain. His shoes and clothing were wet through and icy cold. They couldn’t imagine where he had been on such a dreadful night. And then they found a lantern, still lighted, and a ladder that had been dragged from its place, and some scattered brushes, and a palette with green and yellow colors mixed on it, and—look out the window, dear, at the last ivy leaf on the wall. Didn’t you wonder why it never fluttered or moved when the wind blew? Ah, darling, it’s Behrman’s masterpiece—he painted it there the night that the last leaf fell.”
candidate.foreshadow_summary: Johnsy fixates on the ivy leaves outside her window, counting them down and believing her life will end when the last leaf falls.
candidate.payoff_summary: After Behrman's death, it is revealed that he painted a convincing last leaf on the wall during the storm, which stayed, giving Johnsy the will to live.

proposed_trigger.description: Behrman, after hearing Johnsy's fantasy and seeing the nearly bare vine during the storm, secretly paints a leaf on the wall overnight.
observable_conditions: Behrman and Sue peer out the window fearfully at the ivy vine (P0036)
Cold rain and snow are falling (P0036)
The next morning one leaf remains (P0040)
Lantern, ladder, brushes, and palette are found (P0055)

candidate.relation_description: The leaf that Johnsy counts down is not a real leaf but Behrman's painting, making his earlier talk of a masterpiece ironic and tragic.
candidate.stage1_rationale: The ivy leaf is a central object introduced early with mysterious properties (why does it seem to control her fate?), and the payoff explains its true nature, satisfying a significant narrative debt.

final_trigger.description: Behrman paints a leaf on the wall during the stormy night after hearing Johnsy's belief, sacrificing himself.
observable_conditions: Lantern, ladder, brushes, and palette found in his room (P0055)
His shoes and clothing were wet through and icy cold (P0055)
The leaf never fluttered or moved when the wind blew (P0055)

verdict.foreshadow_description: Johnsy counts falling ivy leaves and believes she will die when the last leaf falls.
verdict.payoff_description: Sue reveals that the last leaf is a painting by Behrman, who died of pneumonia after painting it in the storm.
verdict.rationale: F introduces the leaf-counting and Johnsy's superstition, creating unresolved tension. P reveals that the leaf was painted by Behrman, explaining why it didn't fall and recontextualizing his earlier mention of a masterpiece. Trigger is the secret painting act, evidenced by discovered items. This is a narrative payback, not a thematic echo.

candidate.foreshadow_text: Old Behrman was a painter who lived on the ground floor beneath them. He was past sixty and had a Michael Angelo’s Moses beard curling down from the head of a satyr along the body of an imp. Behrman was a failure in art. Forty years he had wielded the brush without getting near enough to touch the hem of his Mistress’s robe. He had been always about to paint a masterpiece, but had never yet begun it. For several years he had painted nothing except now and then a daub in the line of commerce or advertising. He earned a little by serving as a model to those young artists in the colony who could not pay the price of a professional. He drank gin to excess, and still talked of his coming masterpiece. For the rest he was a fierce little old man, who scoffed terribly at softness in any one, and who regarded himself as especial mastiff-in-waiting to protect the two young artists in the studio above.
candidate.payoff_text: “I have something to tell you, white mouse,” she said. “Mr. Behrman died of pneumonia to-day in the hospital. He was ill only two days. The janitor found him on the morning of the first day in his room downstairs helpless with pain. His shoes and clothing were wet through and icy cold. They couldn’t imagine where he had been on such a dreadful night. And then they found a lantern, still lighted, and a ladder that had been dragged from its place, and some scattered brushes, and a palette with green and yellow colors mixed on it, and—look out the window, dear, at the last ivy leaf on the wall. Didn’t you wonder why it never fluttered or moved when the wind blew? Ah, darling, it’s Behrman’s masterpiece—he painted it there the night that the last leaf fell.”
candidate.foreshadow_summary: Behrman is portrayed as a failed artist who has talked for years about painting a masterpiece but never started, with a blank canvas in his room.
candidate.payoff_summary: The painted last leaf is called Behrman's masterpiece, finally created in a selfless act that ends his life.

proposed_trigger.description: Behrman learns of Johnsy's conviction about the last leaf and decides to act, painting the leaf during the stormy night.
observable_conditions: Sue tells Behrman about Johnsy's fancy (P0031)
Behrman's emotional reaction and agreement to pose (P0032-P0035)
Stormy night (P0036)
Discovery of painting materials (P0055)

candidate.relation_description: The early emphasis on Behrman's unrealized masterpiece is fulfilled by his final, sacrificial painting, transforming a running joke into a tragic heroism.
candidate.stage1_rationale: While the masterpiece motif is clearly set up, its connection to the leaf might be seen as a thematic echo rather than a direct cause-effect; however, it is a strong delayed setup-payoff pair.

final_trigger.description: Behrman learns of Johnsy's conviction about the last leaf and decides to act, painting the leaf during the stormy night.
observable_conditions: Sue tells Behrman about Johnsy's fancy (P0031)
Behrman's emotional reaction and agreement to pose (P0032-P0035)
Stormy night (P0036)
Discovery of painting materials (P0055)

verdict.foreshadow_description: Behrman is introduced as a failed painter who constantly talks about his coming masterpiece but has never started it, setting up an unfulfilled promise.
verdict.payoff_description: Behrman's death reveals that he painted the last leaf on the wall during the stormy night, fulfilling his masterpiece at the cost of his life.
verdict.rationale: The early emphasis on Behrman's unrealized masterpiece is directly resolved when Sue reveals the painted leaf as his masterpiece, transforming a running joke into tragic heroism. This is a clear narrative payoff, not mere thematic echo, as it provides new information that recontextualizes the setup and completes a character arc.

candidate.foreshadow_text:
Mr. Pneumonia was not what you would call a chivalric old gentleman. A mite of a little woman with blood thinned by California zephyrs was hardly fair game for the red-fisted, short-breathed old duffer. But Johnsy he smote; and she lay, scarcely moving, on her painted iron bedstead, looking through the small Dutch window-panes at the blank side of the next brick house.

One morning the busy doctor invited Sue into the hallway with a shaggy, gray eyebrow.

“She has one chance in—let us say, ten,” he said, as he shook down the mercury in his clinical thermometer. “And that chance is for her to want to live. This way people have of lining-up on the side of the undertaker makes the entire pharmacopeia look silly. Your little lady has made up her mind that she’s not going to get well. Has she anything on her mind?”

“She—she wanted to paint the Bay of Naples some day,” said Sue.

“Paint?—bosh! Has she anything on her mind worth thinking about twice—a man, for instance?”

“A man?” said Sue, with a jew’s-harp twang in her voice. “Is a man worth—but, no, doctor; there is nothing of the kind.”

“Well, it is the weakness, then,” said the doctor. “I will do all that science, so far as it may filter through my efforts, can accomplish. But whenever my patient begins to count the carriages in her funeral procession I subtract 50 per cent. from the curative power of medicines. If you will get her to ask one question about the new winter styles in cloak sleeves I will promise you a one-in-five chance for her, instead of one in ten.”

After the doctor had gone Sue went into the workroom and cried a Japanese napkin to a pulp. Then she swaggered into Johnsy’s room with her drawing board, whistling ragtime.

Johnsy lay, scarcely making a ripple under the bedclothes, with her face toward the window. Sue stopped whistling, thinking she was asleep.

She arranged her board and began a pen-and-ink drawing to illustrate a magazine story. Young artists must pave their way to Art by drawing pictures for magazine stories that young authors write to pave their way to Literature.

As Sue was sketching a pair of elegant horseshow riding trousers and a monocle on the figure of the hero, an Idaho cowboy, she heard a low sound, several times repeated. She went quickly to the bedside.

Johnsy’s eyes were open wide. She was looking out the window and counting—counting backward.

“Twelve,” she said, and a little later “eleven;” and then “ten,” and “nine;” and then “eight” and “seven,” almost together.

Sue looked solicitously out the window. What was there to count? There was only a bare, dreary yard to be seen, and the blank side of the brick house twenty feet away. An old, old ivy vine, gnarled and decayed at the roots, climbed half way up the brick wall. The cold breath of autumn had stricken its leaves from the vine until its skeleton branches clung, almost bare, to the crumbling bricks.
candidate.payoff_text: “I have something to tell you, white mouse,” she said. “Mr. Behrman died of pneumonia to-day in the hospital. He was ill only two days. The janitor found him on the morning of the first day in his room downstairs helpless with pain. His shoes and clothing were wet through and icy cold. They couldn’t imagine where he had been on such a dreadful night. And then they found a lantern, still lighted, and a ladder that had been dragged from its place, and some scattered brushes, and a palette with green and yellow colors mixed on it, and—look out the window, dear, at the last ivy leaf on the wall. Didn’t you wonder why it never fluttered or moved when the wind blew? Ah, darling, it’s Behrman’s masterpiece—he painted it there the night that the last leaf fell.”
candidate.foreshadow_summary: The view from Johnsy's window is a bare yard and the blank side of a brick house, with an old ivy vine climbing half way up, mostly bare due to autumn.
candidate.payoff_summary: The painted last leaf is on the brick wall, taking advantage of the blank wall as a canvas, and its realistic attachment to the vine branch explains why Johnsy never suspected it was painted.

proposed_trigger.description: Behrman uses the blank brick wall as his canvas on the stormy night to paint the leaf.
observable_conditions: The wall is described as blank (P0018)
The storm provides cover (P0036)
The leaf is seen clinging high up (P0040)
Ladder and lantern found (P0055)

candidate.relation_description: The spatial description of the window and wall sets the stage for Behrman to execute his painting, making the payoff spatially plausible.
candidate.stage1_rationale: While the blank wall is a necessary condition, it is a very subtle setup and could be considered merely descriptive rather than a deliberate foreshadowing element. However, for a high-recall step, it qualifies.

final_trigger.description: Behrman uses the blank brick wall as his canvas on the stormy night to paint the leaf.
observable_conditions: The blank brick wall facing the window (P0018)
The stormy night implied by Behrman's wet, icy cold clothes (P0055)
The last ivy leaf seen clinging high up (counted by Johnsy, referenced in P0055)
Ladder, lantern, brushes, and palette found (P0055)

verdict.foreshadow_description: Johnsy is shown lying ill and staring through the window at the blank brick wall and the decaying ivy vine, establishing the physical canvas and the context of counting leaves.
verdict.payoff_description: Sue reveals that Behrman painted the last leaf on the wall during the stormy night, which prevented it from falling and saved Johnsy's life, thereby repurposing the previously described blank wall.
verdict.rationale: The setup introduces the blank wall and vine as specific narrative elements without resolving their significance. The payoff provides new information that directly fulfills the setup by revealing Behrman's act, which was made possible by the described spatial and weather conditions. The trigger is observable through the evidence of the painted leaf, the storm, and the found objects. All validity criteria are satisfied.
